event_blocks.summary: The segment begins with the 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber' title card and a male voice announcing, "On Prime Crime." The scene shifts to a desert-style home where a distressed male voice on a 911 call reports, "I just had a 12-year-old boy show up here at my front door asking for help. We need the cops here as soon as possible." This is followed by a nighttime scene with flashing police lights, as a narrator identifies the situation as stemming from a "popular YouTuber" and a "911 call heard around the world." A montage depicts a woman with blonde hair in various settings, including shopping with children, holding peanut butter jars, posing in holiday hats, and speaking professionally, while a female voice comments, "If you're in a position of power and you turn your power and give it over to the kids, that is control." The narrator introduces "her partner in crime," and a different female voice identifies herself as Jody Hilderbrand at the "second annual Women's Retreat." Another male voice expresses shock, stating, "I'm as shocked as anybody. Her and Ruby were just the two wrong things to come together." As the narrator previews a story "that has layers... upon layers," the video transitions to bodycam footage showing a man in a white shirt and red shorts on a porch, with an officer's voice heard saying, "She can stay right here on the porch so you can see her," and asking, "What are you afraid of, sir?" The scene then shows the woman with blonde hair in a striped prison uniform being escorted by a law enforcement officer in a courtroom, followed by a woman in a blue hoodie cooking in a modern kitchen. A female voice describes Jody's approach to "therapeutic modalities" as "like a frog being boiled in water," starting with cold water and slowly turning up the temperature, before the segment concludes with a close-up of bubbling liquid in a pot and a return to the 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME' title card with swelling background music.

event_blocks.summary: The 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber' title sequence, characterized by a dark, textured background, fragmented black-and-white images, bold red and white text, and suspenseful music, transitions to Jesse Weber, dressed in a navy blue suit and white shirt, within a modern studio featuring brick walls, a spiral staircase, and a courthouse graphic. Weber introduces himself and the show, stating, "Hey there everybody, I'm Jesse Weber and welcome to Prime Crime. This is where we break down the most compelling true crime cases in America," before setting up a story that "had the entire nation buzzing" and "all began with one chilling 911 phone call." An aerial view of a remote desert landscape with a modern house appears as a male voice from a 911 call recounts, "I just had a 12-year-old boy show up here at my front door asking for help." Jesse Weber, narrating, specifies the context: "August 30th, 2023, Ivins, Utah. What you're hearing is not your typical 911 phone call, but this isn't your typical case. And it all begins with one woman." The scene then focuses on a woman with blonde hair, first seen in a floral top and maroon cardigan in a brightly lit room with a colorful geometric background, earnestly stating, "Worshipping your child will be placing your child's desires over responsibility." A subsequent shot shows the same woman in a black-and-white checkered shirt, smiling broadly with her hands raised in a shrugging gesture. Weber identifies her as "Ruby" as the segment concludes with a yellow and black 'Morgan & Morgan' sponsorship tag superimposed on the desert footage.

event_blocks.summary: Ruby Frankie, a woman with blonde hair, initially described herself as a 'crazy mom' who traditionally dressed her children in red, white, and blue, a practice dating back to their infancy, while a young boy in a white shirt laughed nearby and a woman in a blue apron baked with a young girl in a yellow dress. KUTV2 News Anchor/Reporter Brian Schnee identified Frankie as a YouTuber with millions of followers for her 'Eight Passengers' channel, which UT Criminal Defense Attorney Jeremy Deus explained was started by Ruby and her husband Kevin as a mommy blog showcasing their family life, initially featuring six children. The narrative highlights a festive family gathering around a Christmas tree with excited screams and cheers as gifts were opened, a dog present, and Frankie commenting on the children's excitement over each other's presents. The family, including Kevin, Julie, Shari, Chad, Ruby, Russell, Abby, and Eve, was shown in winter clothing, and a scene depicted a woman with a blue backpack and a child boarding a white van in Florida, with the children unaware of the trip. Later, Frankie, in a black top, addressed children about dinner and warned against 'silly business' amidst background chatter and a distinct cry/scream. A group of children, including a toddler in overalls and a girl in a pink dress, excitedly gathered around a table in a kitchen for a gift-shaped cake, decorated in green with yellow and blue icing, which was cut amidst exclamations about its hidden color. A young boy in a red shirt and black jacket was shown preparing a meal, opening the refrigerator and retrieving a container for a stove, while an adult female voice asked him about dating, to which he responded he felt too young at 13.

event_blocks.summary: A blonde-haired woman in a kitchen, initially serious then animated, addresses the camera, saying, "talk to me, right? You ever want to," and concluding with "Good talk." Brian Schnee, a KUTV2 News Anchor/Reporter in a gray suit, reports from a studio with a red backdrop about the 'Eight Passengers' and 'Frankie family' YouTube channel, noting their 'over 2 million subscribers' and their influence on parents seeking social media advice, accompanied by light background music. A young boy in a red shirt and black jacket opens a refrigerator, takes out a container, and moves towards a stove with a red pot, seemingly helping with cooking, as a child's voice exclaims, "Why you yell at me?" followed by an adult female laugh and a shushing sound. A young girl in a blue hoodie snacks at a table, and the blonde-haired woman's voice humorously remarks, "The fact that I yell at my kids is a secret. We don't want our viewers to know." The scene shifts to the woman and a young girl in an orange shirt by a piano, with the girl playing, after which the woman gives a piggyback ride to a young boy in a blue shirt, both laughing. A male voice narrates, "However, these videos started to take a dark turn." The woman, now in a car wearing a light blue scarf, smiles and talks, followed by a close-up of a young girl in a white dress with a blue floral pattern holding a blue plush toy. A different woman's voice reflects, detailing how "Eve did not pack a lunch today and can I bring a lunch over to the school?" and that "her teacher is uncomfortable with her being hungry." The blonde-haired woman reappears in the car, now in a yellow beanie and orange top, speaking to the camera, as the reflective voice continues, "I responded and just said, Eve is responsible for making her lunches in the morning. So the natural act..." The video then shows a young girl in a blue and white dress with a horse print pulling a white shopping bag on a patio, skateboarding in a store aisle, and sitting in a purple folding chair at a grassy field, pointing excitedly. The final shot shows the blonde-haired woman in the car, still wearing the yellow beanie, gesturing near her face while speaking.

event_blocks.summary: A blonde-haired woman in a light pink shirt and a young person in a 'We Run Things' white shirt discuss a past incident where 'Russell' was left to 'dunk a basketball,' with the young person initially finding it amusing before admitting it wasn't funny after the woman's pointed remark about needing 'longer without a bedroom' seven months prior. A male narrator explains this led to online criticism, exemplified by an Instagram post from 'moms_of_truth' featuring a family photo with negative comments like 'I hope you go to hell!!!'. The narrative shifts to a Change.org petition, 'Get 8Passengers (Ruby Franke) Under Investigation For Child Abuse,' which has nearly 18,000 signatures and accuses Ruby Franke of neglect and abuse, citing 'obvious concerns.' A brief, chaotic scene shows a woman in a blue shirt running with a megaphone and a dog in a bedroom before returning to the initial pair. The segment concludes in a dimly lit bedroom where a young girl in a light blue nightgown looks up with concern as a distressed woman's voice questions, 'Eve, this is my tape. What are you doing? What are you doing to this poor baby?' followed by a dog barking and the woman asking, 'Should I tape you?'. Earlier, a young girl held a 'Trix' cereal box on a polka-dotted bed as Carl Andrease discussed methods 'to make you sleep.' Carl Andrease, a YouTube vlogger, expressed concern about 'all the kids get hurt on the YouTube channels' and recounted disturbing observations of 'split fingers and bloody noses' and parents giving children 'Tylenol PMs' because they 'won't go to bed.' He also mentioned a half-hour 'conversation with Ruby... about parenting' where he perceived 'red flags throughout all of that.' A woman in an orange fleece jacket and wide-brimmed hat addressed children on a wooden dock. Shari Franke, identified as having blonde, wavy hair, introduced herself and stated, 'My four younger siblings are living in Springville,' adding that neighbors reported the children were 'left home alone.' This was preceded by a report of 'over the years there were a number of calls to Ruby's home in Springville... because the kids were left alone,' and followed by a shot of children lounging on a couch in a dimly lit living room and a young girl with long blonde hair and glasses speaking from a car.

event_blocks.summary: On August 30, 2023, a 911 call was placed by an older male neighbor reporting a distressed, emaciated 12-year-old boy at his door in Ivins, Utah, who had escaped a nearby modern house in a desert landscape, exhibiting tape marks on his legs and visible wounds, and expressing fear. News anchor Brian Schnee reported that the boy, along with his younger sibling, were children of Ruby Franke, and that one child had escaped to the neighbor's house. Simultaneously, Springville Police and the Utah County Major Crimes Task Force responded to Ruby Franke's home in Springville, executing a DCFS warrant to take custody of two outstanding minor children, kicking down the door and clearing the house, but finding the children were not present. Later, officers, including Det. Mulford, located two girls, identified as Ruby Franke's daughters, at the home of Pam Bodtcher, President of Connexions Foundation, and her husband Roy. Pam and Roy were initially confused and questioned the officers' authority, but Pam was taken into custody after officers confirmed they had a warrant specifically for her and one of the children. Pam explained that Ruby had contacted her due to a family emergency, asking her to pick up the girls, and that the girls had been cleaning her house. Bodycam footage showed Pam's distressed state while being questioned, and Roy's protective stance, expressing confusion and fear about the situation. Jesse Weber, a legal commentator, noted Pam's confusion and the officers' lack of facts, stating Pam had not been charged. Jeremy Deus, a UT Criminal Defense Attorney, described the neighbor's call as heartbreaking and noted Pam's husband was described as 'stand-offish, obstinate.' The children were found unharmed at Pam Bodtcher's home, with one officer asking Pam if she needed medical attention and if there was a reason for her fear, to which she responded, 'It's us.' The narrator commented on the children's standoffish behavior towards the police.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a black screen displaying a 911 call transcript dated August 30, 2023, as a distressed, older male voice, the 911 caller, states, 'I just had a 12-year-old boy show up here at my front door asking for help.' The scene transitions to an aerial view of a remote desert landscape featuring a modern house identified by a 'TAWFOO COURT' sign, while KUTV2 News Anchor/Reporter Brian Schnee, seen later in a studio, explains that Santa Clara Ivins Public Safety received a call about a situation at a home in Ivins, Utah, identified as the home of Jodi Hildebrandt. A white Santa Clara Police SUV with flashing lights is shown driving through this desert environment. Brian Schnee continues, stating that two of Ruby Franke's younger children, aged 9 and 12, were at the home, and one was able to escape to a neighbor's house. Photos appear of a woman with blonde hair (implicitly Ruby Franke) with children: first in a brown hat and plaid jacket with two children in front of pumpkins, then hugging a boy in a red-and-white striped shirt in a tropical setting. The 911 caller's voice describes the boy as 'emaciated, he’s got tape around his legs, he’s hungry, and he’s thirsty,' adding that the boy asked them to call the police and is 'very afraid.' Brian Schnee then appears in a studio with a red backdrop, reflecting on the haunting nature of the 911 call and describing the caller as 'the neighbor, an older gentleman, extremely compassionate.' The isolated desert house is shown again from multiple angles. The video concludes with more photos of the woman with blonde hair: reading a book with a boy in a Superman hoodie on a couch, and hugging two children in front of a stone wall, as the 911 caller's voiceover states, 'This kid has obviously been... He’s been detained,' and 'He’s very covered in wounds.'

The segment opens with a still photo of a blonde woman in a red-and-white striped shirt, smiling with two children against a stone wall, dated 'AUGUST 30, 2023'. Overlaid is a 911 call transcript, which is simultaneously heard as a distressed male voice reports, "He’s been detained. He’s obviously covered in wounds. We need the cops here as soon as possible." The scene transitions to Jeremy Deus, a UT Criminal Defense Attorney, in a checkered shirt and tie, speaking from an office, describing the "heartbreaking" 911 call where a "12-year-old son" escaped through a basement window to a neighbor's house, asking for "a drink and food." An aerial shot of a modern gray house in a desert landscape, again with the 911 transcript overlay, accompanies Jeremy Deus's continued narration about the neighbor observing "ligature marks" and the child being "emaciated and malnourished," indicating the child "was tied up for quite some time." Quick cuts then show a young boy in a red shirt running on a patio, another boy holding a handwritten '$55' sign, and a blonde woman in a blue shirt laughing and playfully touching a young boy’s face in a kitchen. A still photo captures the same woman in an orange fleece jacket and wide-brimmed hat, laughing with a boy on a wooden porch. Brian Schnee, a KUTV2 News Anchor/Reporter, then speaks from a studio, detailing how "Russell was the one they found first," describing "bruising and blistering" from "cayenne pepper and honey" applied to wounds. The video briefly shows a young girl pulling toys from a dresser drawer before concluding with the blonde woman reading a book with two children on an outdoor patio, as Schnee notes less was described about "Eve" than "Russell."

The segment opens with a wide shot of a large, two-story beige house under a bright blue sky, as a male narrator explains that Russell and his sister Eve were taken to the hospital for malnutrition and injuries, prompting the question: "Where are Ruby's other minor children?" Bodycam footage from the Springville Police Department, timestamped August 30, 2023, shows an officer aiming a firearm toward the house, with a white van in the driveway. The narrator details that officers from Springville Police and Utah County Major Crimes Task Force went to "Ruby Franke's home in Springville" to locate "two outstanding children that were under the age of 18." A family photo of two smiling young girls and other family members briefly appears before the bodycam returns, showing multiple officers in tactical gear approaching and then kicking in the front door, as the narrator confirms they "kick the door down" and "clear the house." Inside, a woman with blonde hair is apprehended, mouth open as if shouting, but the narrator states, "They realized the children are not there." The scene cuts to a woman with blonde hair in a maroon beanie speaking animatedly in a car, followed by a photo of a woman giving a thumbs-up in a kitchen. Another bodycam segment shows an officer walking toward a different house, with on-screen subtitles indicating a search for a minor sibling and a DCFS warrant for custody. Distorted audio, likely from Det. Mulford, confirms, "There's one other sibling that we're still looking for, considered still in dangerous and DCFS has a warrant to take them both into custody." The segment concludes with a photo of two women laughing in a car, as subtitles identify Det. Mulford and state "Pam was seen picking up a minor from a rec center," further supported by the distorted audio stating, "Pam was seen picking up [inaudible] from the Springville Rec Center around 1:15."

The video opens with real bodycam footage from August 30, 2023, displaying a timestamp and 'REAL BODYCAM FOOTAGE' overlay. Officer Hawkins, in a dark uniform with visible tattoos, asks a man identified as Roy, in a white t-shirt and red shorts at a stone-walled doorway, “Is Pam in today?” Roy identifies her as his wife. A woman's voice (Pam) is heard saying “Good” before Officer Hawkins greets her. The scene cuts to a still image of Pam Bodtcher, identified as President of Connexions Foundation, as a male narrator (Brian Schnee) explains that “Pam Botcher is the third member of Connections Classroom” and that “the two girls were found at Pam Botcher's home. They were unharmed.” The footage returns to the doorway where Pam, now visible in a yellow tie-dye shirt and holding a phone, appears. Roy questions the officer, “You have a warrant?” The officer confirms, “Yeah, we do,” and after Roy asks if they are physically seizing anyone, a voice shouts, “Under that doorway!” Brian Schnee's narration continues, describing how Pam and her husband are “stunned” and “don't understand what is going on” when police arrive. Back at the residential porch, the officer, with a yellow tool on his belt, tells Pam and Roy, “We'll go ahead and get the copy of the warrant for you, okay?” Pam, appearing cautious, insists, “You have a warrant for my house?” The officer confirms it is “specifically for you... for you and the child.” Roy asks, “What is going on?” The officer then reaches out to take Pam’s hand, and she pulls back slightly. The scene briefly cuts to Brian Schnee, a KUTV2 News Anchor/Reporter in a gray suit, speaking directly to the camera in a studio with a red backdrop, before returning to the doorway as his narration explains that police “place her into custody... putting cuffs on her and putting her in one of the patrol cars.”

The minute opens with bodycam footage showing a woman in a yellow tie-dye shirt, identified as Pam, at a doorway, interacting with a law enforcement officer whose tattooed arm is visible. A narrator sets the scene, questioning Pam's knowledge of Ruby, the girls' presence, and how Pam initially had them. The scene shifts to a still photo of a family with four children, overlaid with text visually identifying Pam as Ruby Franke’s daughter and a friend of the family. Pam's voice is then heard clarifying that she is 'a friend of ours' and that one of the girls is 'Ruby Frankie's daughter,' explaining she didn't make them scrub the floor but they 'vacuumed and stuff' because she had company coming, and that Ruby was traveling from Costa Rica. An officer acknowledges with 'Okay,' and Pam emphasizes the children's actions were 'of her own free will.' A narrator highlights Pam's repeated statements about the children 'just doing some cleaning for me' and eating 'some ice cream.' The footage returns to bodycam views, showing Pam seated in a police vehicle, and later standing by a vehicle, speaking to an officer. The officer's voice is heard confirming details, asking if Ruby contacted her around noon. Pam recounts how Ruby contacted her due to a 'family emergency,' asking her to pick up the girls, and Pam agreed, stating she needed 'some help cleaning.' She explains she informed the pool staff of the emergency, and told the children their mom needed help and that Ruby would be at Ace Hardware, recounting, 'Your mom said, 'Would you come help me clean today?' She said, 'Okay.'' The bodycam footage, displaying a timestamp of '2023-08-30 18:27:15 -0600' and 'REAL BODYCAM FOOTAGE' text, shows the lighting shift from indoor to outdoor daylight, maintaining a tense atmosphere during the police interactions. Interspersed are calm, domestic scenes: a young girl in a floral dress, and two girls in red hanging chairs on an outdoor patio, one eating ice cream, with a large white dog walking nearby, contrasting with the police questioning.

The segment opens with a man identified as Jesse Weber, in a gray suit, speaking directly to the camera in a studio with a red wall and window, his clear, articulate voice explaining that Pam was extremely confused and the officer lacked facts, noting that Pam has not been charged and he's curious if she was questioned about Ruby and Jody. The scene then transitions to real bodycam footage from August 30, 2023, showing a distressed woman, Pam, in a yellow tie-dye shirt, sitting inside a police vehicle. A calm male law enforcement officer, whose tattooed arm is visible, asks if he can talk to her by his car, to which Pam responds with a distressed, "little out of." A firm male voice, identified as Roy, then suggests, "she can go in and lock the door," which the officer repeats. The officer then asks if another officer goes with her, to which Roy firmly replies, "No." The footage briefly cuts to a cheerful photo of three women laughing on a boat and a selfie-style shot of two women smiling in a cozy living room, before returning to a man in a checkered shirt and tie, identified as Jeremy Deus, speaking in an office setting. Jeremy's professional voice discusses police finding children at Pam's house and talking to Pam's "stand-offish, obstinate" husband. The bodycam footage resumes, showing Roy, in a white shirt and red shorts, standing at his front porch with arms crossed alongside a young girl, as the officer suggests Pam "can stay right here on the porch so you can see her." Roy's voice agrees, "See her. Uh-huh. Absolutely." The officer then gestures, asking Roy, "What are you afraid of, sir?" to which Roy, appearing concerned, replies, "I'm, I don't know what's going on." Throughout the bodycam segments, the 'REAL BODYCAM FOOTAGE' overlay, timestamp, and 'truthfinder' sponsor logo are visible.

The video opens with real bodycam footage from August 30, 2023, showing Roy, a man in a white t-shirt and red shorts, standing at his front porch with arms crossed. He appears defensive and concerned as he speaks to an officer, stating firmly, "I don't know what's going on," and expressing his desire to prevent Springville from driving up and "whisk[ing] her away," emphasizing his role in trying to protect her from a bad situation. The porch setting, with its black door, yellow wreath, stone walls, and rocking chair, is visible. An external narrator then comments, "And it's not just Botcher's husband who seems resistant." The scene shifts to a woman, identified as Pam, sitting on the rocking chair, her face blurred for privacy. She appears distressed, with hands clasped and legs drawn up, as a female officer's voice asks, "Do you need medical attention at all?" and "Is there a reason you're so scared right now?" Pam quietly responds, "It's us." The officer continues with a reassuring tone, "My intention is not to scare you," and asks, "Are you okay? Or is there something that I can do to make you not feel as scared right now?" The footage is overlaid with timestamps and 'AXON BODY 3' identifiers. The segment concludes with a still photo of a smiling blonde woman with sunglasses surrounded by four children, while the narrator adds, "The children don't really want to engage with police. They're standoffish," suggesting a family context to the unfolding situation.

event_blocks.summary: Bodycam footage from August 30, 2023, shows Officer Hawkins and a DCFS staffer discussing the melancholy state of children, with the staffer noting the older sister mentioned things might be rough, leading to the children being taken into state custody and Ruby Frankie and Jody Hildebrant being arrested and charged with six counts of aggravated child abuse each for Russell and Eve's injuries. A montage of personal photos, including two women smiling in holiday hats in a desert and children playing in a backyard, accompanies narration about the arrests, which then transitions to a YouTube playlist titled 'YouTube Mom Child Abuse Case – UT v. Ruby Franke.' A man in a checkered shirt explains the legal ramifications of aggravated child abuse, a second-degree felony with a one to 15-year sentence, potentially related to emotional abuse or severe malnutrition. The narrator states the arrests caused a firestorm, and Jesse Weber, in a gray suit, addresses the camera, noting global interest in how the events unfolded, concluding with a photo of two women laughing in a car under a 'COMING UP' banner. The narrative shifts to a family meal scene with tense music as Ruby Frankie and Jody Hildebrant's relationship is described as messy and unbelievable. A police car is seen on a suburban street as Ruby Frankie's family reacts to the arrest, with an Instagram story from Ruby's adult daughter, Sharry, expressing relief that justice is being served after years of trying to alert authorities. Visuals of a young woman in a car, children in formal attire, a woman on a bed with children, and children in a wooded area accompany speculation about the family dynamic and the exhaustion and fear of the family, ending with a family in winter clothing and a 'COMING UP' graphic for 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME.' A 'COMING UP' teaser for 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber' previews Ruby Frankie making a decision that upends the case and reveals secrets about the children, accompanied by suspenseful music and visuals of a woman in winter clothing and a blonde woman with children in a kitchen. A legal document excerpt mentioning 'physical torture' and 'denied adequate water' is shown, followed by a voice expressing shock at the details. Jesse Weber, in a studio with headphones and a Shure microphone against a rusted metal backdrop, emphasizes the importance of knowing one's associates and introduces Truthfinder.com as a safety service, with a graphic displaying the sponsor URL. A 'People Search Service' webpage is shown as Jesse Weber explains Truthfinder's function in providing public record information, illustrated by a smartphone using the Truthfinder app. Screen recordings of the Truthfinder website demonstrate its search functionalities, with Jesse Weber explaining how to access phone numbers, addresses, associates, and criminal convictions, and identify sex offenders. He is seen again in the studio, emphasizing Truthfinder's utility for online dating and background checks. A close-up of a hand navigating the Truthfinder app on a smartphone shows 'Location History' and 'Criminal and Traffic Records.' A 'Report Summary' for 'Trudy T Truthfinder' is displayed, and Jesse Weber announces a 50% off promotional offer for confidential background reports, directing viewers to the sponsor URL. The segment concludes with a title card for 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber' and a courtroom scene featuring Lamar J. Winward, Ruby Franke’s attorney, at a podium, with the Truthfinder logo and URL consistently displayed.

raw_segments: The video opens with real bodycam footage from August 30, 2023, showing Officer Hawkins, in a dark police uniform with a badge, heard explaining to a DCFS staffer beside a black pickup truck that the "girls have been melancholy... they're just sitting on the porch and really said no word to us. They whisper stuff to each other." The DCFS staffer, with a tattooed arm, is heard responding that the "older sister was saying that it might be rough." A male narrator's voice then states, "The children are ultimately taken into state custody. Ruby Frankie and Jody Hildebrant are arrested and charged with six counts of aggravated child abuse each with respect to Russell and Eve's injuries." This narration plays over a montage of personal photos: two women smiling in holiday hats in a desert, a young girl riding on a boy’s shoulders, a man in a checkered shirt speaking in an office, two women smiling in an office setting, a young girl with a blue balloon, and children playing in a backyard. The scene transitions to a YouTube playlist titled 'YouTube Mom Child Abuse Case – UT v. Ruby Franke'. The man in the checkered shirt, previously seen in the montage, is then heard discussing the legal aspects of the case, stating, "You're talking about serious bodily injury and you get to the aggravated child abuse portion of it, which is a second-degree felony. That's a one to 15-year sentence, which is what these charges ended up being. Sometimes that's related to emotional abuse. Sometimes that's related to severe malnutrition. It can be a combination of them." The male narrator's voice returns, declaring, "The arrest of Frankie and Hildebrant sets off a firestorm." Finally, Jesse Weber, in a gray suit, is seen and heard speaking directly to the camera with expressive gestures, adding, "It really gained the interest from people around the world about how this could take place and how these two lives..." The segment concludes with a photo of two women laughing in a car, one wearing sunglasses, with a 'COMING UP' banner.

The segment opens with a warmly lit scene of a family, including adults and children, enjoying a casual meal around a dining table, featuring lemonade and chips, as a tense, investigative background music begins. A narrator introduces the complex relationship between Ruby Frankie and Jody Hildebrant, describing it as incredibly messy and unbelievable, like something from a movie, 'the worst of the worst.' The visuals transition to a still photo of two women smiling in a laundry room, followed by an outdoor scene of a police car on a suburban street with officers and a woman, coinciding with the narrator stating that 'Ruby Frankie's own family reacted to the arrest.' An Instagram story screenshot from 'officialsharifranke' then appears, with the narrator identifying the poster as Ruby Frankie's adult daughter, Sharry, and reading her statement expressing relief that 'justice is being served' after years of trying to tell the police and CPS. Subsequent visuals show a young woman with long brown hair talking in a car, a group of children in formal attire, a woman in a purple top on a bed surrounded by playing children, and children in a wooded area, all while the narrator continues to ponder the 'hard to understand' family dynamic, noting that only Sharry's social media posts are seen, and speculating that the family must be 'exhausted' and 'frightened.' The segment concludes with a family in winter clothing posing in front of a snowy house, overlaid with a 'COMING UP' graphic for 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME', as the narrator's commentary trails off, indicating this is 'only part' of the story.

The segment opens with a 'COMING UP' teaser for 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber,' as a male voice, later identified as Jesse Weber, dramatically states, "the story, when we return, Ruby Frankie makes a decision that upends the case, and secrets are revealed about what actually happened to the Frankie children," accompanied by suspenseful music. Visually, a woman in a winter hat and coat is shown, followed by a family scene in a modern kitchen where a blonde woman interacts with two children. The scene then transitions to a legal document highlighting phrases like 'physical torture' and 'denied adequate water,' which the audio implies are details from the "Ruby Frankie story." Another male voice then shares, "I think I share the same reaction that a lot of people share. I'm as shocked as anybody as you read these," before Jesse Weber's voice returns, urging, "Stay with us," as the music continues and fades. The show's title card, 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber,' appears. Jesse Weber then appears in a studio, wearing headphones and speaking into a Shure microphone against a rusted metal backdrop, gesturing expressively. He addresses the audience, stating, "Hey, so we're going to get back to Prime Crime in just a minute, but what is the Ruby Frankie story make very clear? It's that you have to know who you're surrounding yourself with, right?" He transitions to a sponsor segment, introducing "truthfinder.com" as a service that can provide "actual safety." A lower-third graphic displays the sponsor URL 'www.truthfinder.com/LCPrimeCrime.' The video then shows a 'People Search Service' webpage with fields for 'First Name' and 'Last Name,' as Jesse Weber explains Truthfinder is "one of the largest public record search services in the entire world" that helps people "learn the facts about the people in their lives." A close-up of a hand using a smartphone to access the Truthfinder app, typing 'John' and 'Smith' into the search fields, illustrates his explanation of how to "type in a name" on their website. The URL 'www.truthfinder.com/LCPrimeCrime' remains visible.

The video opens with screen recordings of the Truthfinder website interface, showcasing its various search functionalities like 'Background Reports,' 'Contact Info,' 'Location History,' 'Criminal and Traffic Records,' and 'Sex Offender Information.' Fields for entering names, cities, and states are displayed, with example data like 'Jesse Smith' and 'San Diego, CA' being input. A navigation menu on the left lists sections such as 'Report Summary,' 'Timeline,' 'Personal Info,' and 'Assets.' A male voice, identified as Jesse Weber, begins to speak, explaining how Truthfinder allows users to access information like phone numbers, addresses, associates, and criminal convictions, and to identify registered sex offenders in an area by typing in an address. Jesse Weber, wearing a dark gray long-sleeve shirt and over-ear headphones, is then seen speaking into a Shure microphone in a studio with textured, rusted metal panels as a backdrop, gesturing expressively as he continues to emphasize Truthfinder's utility, particularly for online dating, stressing the importance of background checks. The video cuts to a close-up of a hand navigating the Truthfinder app on a smartphone, tapping through 'Location History' and 'Criminal and Traffic Records.' The screen returns to the website, showing a 'Report Summary' for 'Trudy T Truthfinder' with an address in San Diego, CA. Jesse Weber then announces a promotional offer for 50% off confidential background reports, directing viewers to 'www.truthfinder.com/LCPrimeCrime'. The segment concludes with a title card for 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber,' followed by a courtroom scene where a man in a blue suit, identified as Lamar J. Winward (Ruby Franke’s attorney), stands at a wooden podium, all set against a backdrop of dramatic background music. Throughout the video, the green and white Truthfinder logo and the URL 'www.truthfinder.com/LCPrimeCrime' are consistently displayed at the bottom of the screen.

event_blocks.summary: In a courtroom, defendant Ruby Franke, wearing a yellow and white top, confirms she can hear and has received case information. The scene shifts to a suburban driveway at sunset, featuring a white van and basketball hoop, as a narrator announces the arrest of Ruby Franke and Jodi Hildebrandt for aggravated child abuse. Ruby Franke is then shown in a living room with a young girl in a pink dress, followed by festive photos of Franke with others, including one in a Santa hat and another family portrait against a stone wall. A voiceover questions the whereabouts of Ruby's husband, Kevin Franke. A young girl in a pink dress walks toward a white van holding a phone. In a garage, a man in a blue cap and gray shirt gestures while speaking to the camera about picking someone up for "Sheri's first European vacation," mentioning "Ruby," "our camera," and "Kevin." A tropical photo shows Ruby Franke and the man smiling. Randy Kester, Kevin Franke’s attorney, states in a law office that his client was "shocked, devastated, hurt," felt "deceived," and hadn't seen his children for "12 months or more." The scene returns to a bald man lying on a living room floor, playfully stepped on by children, while another child uses a laptop on a couch. Later, outside an airport terminal, a bald man in a blue suit embraces a woman with blonde hair in a maroon top, as a voice describes Kevin's love for his family. In a sunlit orchard, a family walks along a path, while the voice explains Ruby felt marital differences. The couple appears indoors, seated side-by-side, as the audio details their separation to save their marriage, with Kevin needing to be away and have no contact with the kids, in conjunction with Jodi Hildebrandt. The man with white hair and beard, narrating, is seen in a formal office. The couple is then shown in a cozy living room, reclining on a couch and sitting close, speaking to the camera, followed by a formal photo. Brian Schnee, a KUTV2 News Anchor/Reporter, explains in a studio that Ruby Franke's separation from Kevin Franke is textbook, similar to other couples experiencing issues with Jodi Hildebrand, who is accused of 'ripping apart' marriages. The couple is seen laughing in a living room, and a woman in a navy blue jacket smiles in a clinic setting. The bald man, now in a gray sweater, speaks to the camera at home, as the narrator details Kevin attending men's groups facilitated by Jodi, where he was downgraded and told he needed to repent by not infecting his family with his problems. The bald man is seen holding a birthday cake and speaking expressively on a couch. The video concludes with a joyful photo of the couple and four children in front of a waterfall.

Ruby Franke.description: Appears in multiple scenes: first in a courtroom setting with a serious expression, wearing a yellow and white top, where she audibly confirms "Yes" twice to questions from a male voice; later in a living room, wearing a light-colored top, speaking to the camera; then in a family portrait in a red-and-white striped shirt; and finally in a tropical photo with sunglasses and a colorful top. Her role evolves from defendant to family member to public figure.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative details the legal proceedings and revelations surrounding Ruby Franke and Jodi Hildebrandt's child abuse case. Initially, a male voice accuses "Miss Hildebrandt" of twisting a quest into something heinous and systematically isolating "Miss Franke" from her family. In court, Ruby Franke pleads "Guilty" to aggravated child abuse, expressing regret. Later, Ruby Franke pleads guilty to four counts of aggravated child abuse, with two dismissed, agreeing to testify against Jody Hildebrandt. Legal analysts discuss the plea agreement, revealing Ruby's admission to kicking Russell and holding his head underwater. The children endured severe abuse, including forced labor, denial of water, and being bound, with victims left "blistering" in the sun for days. Ruby Franke, a former YouTuber, explains controlling behavior as a parent giving power to children. Jodi Hildebrandt also pleads "Guilty" to aggravated child abuse, admitting to forcing a child to jump into a cactus multiple times. The legal process highlights the prosecution's effort to secure cooperation against co-defendants, with Ruby's plea and testimony complicating Jodi's case. The video intersperses these legal developments with personal photos and heartwarming family moments, creating a stark contrast with the horrific details of the abuse.

Franke's voice then takes over, stating, "For the past four years, I've chosen to follow counsel and guidance that has led me into a dark delusion," explaining how her "distorted version of reality went largely unchecked" as she isolated herself from anyone who challenged her, believing the world was an "evil place" filled with "cops who control, hospitals that injure, government agencies that brainwash, husbands who refuse to protect, and children who need..." before her speech is cut off. event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with Jodi Hildebrandt, initially seen smiling indoors in a blue shirt, then transitions to a formal courtroom where she, identified as 'JODI HILDEBRANDT DEFENDANT' under a 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME' banner and wearing a gray and white striped prison uniform, walks with her attorney, a man in a gray suit. Interspersed home footage shows a young girl in a pink shirt and a young boy in a green and white striped shirt receiving gentle touches on the chin. In the courtroom, Jodi, in profile and looking down somberly, expresses her love for the children and her desire for their physical and emotional healing, explaining her decision not to go to trial to spare them further emotional distress, hoping they "will heal and move forward to have beautiful lives." A brief clip shows Jodi smiling in a pink jacket, holding a 'Jodi Hildebrandt ConneXions Masterclass' notebook, prompting a narrator's skepticism. Jodi's niece, Jessi Hildebrandt, with short platinum blonde hair and a neck tattoo, appears against a blue backdrop, critically stating that Jodi's claims of caring for the children's healing are unbelievable after accusations of child torture, and that Jodi's attempts to save face are a "manipulation tactic." Jessi reveals she has been trying to speak about this for "almost 15 years" without being heard. The scene returns to the solemn, wood-paneled courtroom with Jodi seated beside her attorney, then standing at the defendant’s table, with law enforcement and a Utah state flag visible. A young boy in a blue shirt rests his head on a woman in a maroon jacket, as a female voice questions Jodi's sudden concern for the children's healing. Adam Steed, a former client, speaks to reporters outdoors, stating that Hildebrandt has shown little remorse, claiming victimhood, and asserting that the proceedings would be "full of lies." This is shown over a courtroom scene with Jodi and her attorney. Domestic footage shows children playing, followed by a male voice stating, "Ms. Hildebrandt, this circumstance is tragic. It's largely, of course, of your making... your conduct in this case was disastrous for these children. Adults are supposed to protect children." The segment concludes with a wider courtroom view showing Jodi and her attorney standing, with law enforcement and a young boy labeled 'KEVIN' in the audience, and a final close-up of Jodi and her attorney looking forward with serious expressions under the Utah state flag.

The scene transitions to a kitchen where a young girl in a pink shirt and another in a patterned top eat snacks at a table with a bald man in a black shirt, who appears to be interacting with them. Jessi Hildebrandt, a woman with short platinum blonde hair and a neck tattoo, then appears speaking directly to the camera against a blue background, her voice becoming the dominant narrative. She speaks emphatically about "narcissistic or psychopathic abuse," stating she "can't explain it" and that "children" lack the "biological mental facilities to understand what's going on." The visual cuts to a young girl in a white dress carrying sandwiches to a group of children sharing them on an orange polka-dotted rug. A still photo of a woman with long blonde hair and three children smiling in a tropical setting is shown, as Jessi's voice continues, asserting that "this is not going away" and "these children are going to deal with this potentially for the rest of their lives." She adds, "This nearly killed me," urging viewers to "trust your instincts" and "build up that muscle of intuition" because "the things that you are feeling are true." The video then shows a young girl in a striped shirt hugging a woman in a checkered shirt who holds a baby in a tiger-striped onesie. Jessi's voice concludes, emphasizing to "trust yourself" so that "people like Jodi and Frankie are not held in positions of power," adding they "can be your neighbors, they can be your aunts, they can be your mothers," as the final segment features a split-screen interview with two women, one in a floral shirt and the other in a purple top, both speaking seriously.

event_blocks.summary: Jesse Weber, dressed in a navy blue suit, stands in a brightly lit studio designed to resemble an urban environment with brick walls and metal railings, a courthouse graphic displayed on a large screen behind him. Identified by a lower-third graphic as 'Jesse Weber, LAW&CRIME NETWORK' and host of 'PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber,' he addresses the camera directly, using expressive hand gestures as he introduces the case of YouTube momfluencer Ruby Frankie and life coach Jodi Hildebrant, detailing their guilty pleas to child abuse despite their claims of child-rearing expertise. Weber discusses the consequences of seeking fame and the realities of online influencers, cautioning viewers to 'be careful who you get advice from.' He concludes his monologue by thanking the audience and signing off, 'That's all we have for you here on Prime Crime, everybody. Thank you so much for joining us. I'm Jesse Weber. And until next time, stay safe.' The video then transitions to a dark blue, textured background with rolling white production credits, accompanied by gentle, reflective instrumental music and the persistent display of the 'PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber' and 'LAW&CRIME' logos in the top corners.

raw_segments: The segment opens with Jesse Weber, a man with dark hair in a navy blue suit over a white shirt, standing in a brightly lit studio designed to resemble an urban environment with brick walls and metal railings. A large screen behind him displays a courthouse graphic, and a lower-third graphic identifies him as 'Jesse Weber, LAW&CRIME NETWORK' and the show as 'PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber.' Concluding his monologue, he states, "That's all we have for you here on Prime Crime, everybody. Thank you so much for joining us. I'm Jesse Weber. And until next time, stay safe." Following his sign-off, the video transitions to a dark blue, textured background where white text rolls through production credits, listing various roles. Throughout this credit sequence, a gentle, reflective instrumental background music plays, and the 'PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber' and 'LAW&CRIME' logos are consistently displayed in the top corners.
